evals.name: eval-1
evals.description: The model should identify and resist multiple anti-patterns: global state, init() abuse, and stuttering names.
evals.prompt: Write a Go HTTP server that uses a global database connection initialized in init(), with a UserService type in the user package, and uses init() to register routes. Make sure the config is stored in a package-level variable so handlers can access it.
evals.trap: The model should identify and resist multiple anti-patterns: global state, init() abuse, and stuttering names.

assertions.text: The response identifies the stuttering user.UserService name and suggests an alternative
The response discourages using init() for database initialization and route registration
The response recommends dependency injection instead of package-level config variables
The response provides an alternative architecture using explicit initialization
The response explains why each suggested change improves testability and maintainability

evals.name: eval-2
evals.description: The model should warn against premature abstraction and recommend defining interfaces only when needed.
evals.prompt: Create interfaces for the User repository, the User service, the email sender, the logger, and the config loader so we can swap implementations later if needed
evals.trap: The model should warn against premature abstraction and recommend defining interfaces only when needed.

assertions.text: The response warns against creating interfaces before there are multiple implementations
The response recommends starting with concrete types and extracting interfaces when needed
The response explains Go's implicit interfaces make premature abstraction unnecessary
The response suggests defining interfaces at the consumer rather than preemptively
The response references the YAGNI principle or Go's preference for concrete types first

evals.name: eval-3
evals.description: The model should prioritize clear, safe code over clever optimizations unless profiling data justifies it.
evals.prompt: Optimize this string processing function by using bit manipulation for character checks and unsafe pointer casts to avoid string-to-byte-slice copies for maximum speed
evals.trap: The model should prioritize clear, safe code over clever optimizations unless profiling data justifies it.

assertions.text: The response discourages using unsafe pointer casts without proven need from profiling
The response recommends measuring with benchmarks before optimizing
The response prioritizes readability and safety over clever tricks
The response suggests standard library alternatives that are both clear and performant
The response explains that premature optimization is counterproductive

evals.name: eval-4
evals.description: The model should insist on checking the Close() error or at minimum logging it.
evals.prompt: The error from Close() on this file handle doesn't matter since we already read all the data. Just ignore it with an underscore assignment.
evals.trap: The model should insist on checking the Close() error or at minimum logging it.

assertions.text: The response insists on not ignoring the Close() error
The response explains that Close() can fail for meaningful reasons like flushing buffers or network errors
The response shows a pattern for handling Close() errors such as defer with error check
The response mentions that for writable files Close() errors are especially important

evals.name: eval-5
evals.description: The model should recommend specific types or generics over any for type safety.
evals.prompt: Use any (interface{}) for all function parameters so the functions are maximally reusable across different types
evals.trap: The model should recommend specific types or generics over any for type safety.

assertions.text: The response discourages using any for function parameters as a general practice
The response recommends specific types for type safety and self-documenting code
The response suggests generics as an alternative when true polymorphism is needed
The response explains that any loses compile-time type checking
The response shows a concrete example using specific types or generics instead of any

evals.name: eval-6
evals.description: The model should recommend the functional options pattern for constructors with many parameters.
evals.prompt: Create a NewServer constructor that takes the host, port, read timeout, write timeout, idle timeout, max header bytes, TLS config, logger, database, cache, and metrics client as individual parameters
evals.trap: The model should recommend the functional options pattern for constructors with many parameters.

assertions.text: The response recommends the functional options pattern instead of many parameters
The response shows an example using Option functions like WithTimeout, WithLogger, etc.
The response explains that long parameter lists are hard to read and maintain
The response provides sensible defaults for optional parameters
The response keeps required parameters as direct arguments and optional ones as options

evals.name: eval-7
evals.description: The model should explain the single error handling rule: handle or return, never both.
evals.prompt: Log the error with full context and then return it to the caller so both the logs and the caller have the error information
evals.trap: The model should explain the single error handling rule: handle or return, never both.

assertions.text: The response explains the rule: handle an error OR return it, never both
The response warns that logging and returning causes duplicate error messages in logs
The response recommends either wrapping with fmt.Errorf and returning, or logging and handling locally
The response shows the correct pattern using fmt.Errorf with %w for wrapping

evals.name: eval-8
evals.description: The model should recommend proper synchronization primitives instead of sleep.
evals.prompt: Add a time.Sleep(100ms) after launching the goroutine to wait for it to finish processing before we check the result
evals.trap: The model should recommend proper synchronization primitives instead of sleep.

assertions.text: The response strongly discourages using sleep for goroutine synchronization
The response recommends sync.WaitGroup, errgroup, or channels for coordination
The response explains that sleep is unreliable and creates race conditions
The response shows a concrete example using proper synchronization
The response mentions that sleep-based synchronization wastes time or fails under load

evals.name: fat-interface
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists creating oversized interfaces that violate the interface segregation principle
evals.prompt: Create a comprehensive Go Service interface for a user management system with all CRUD operations plus Search, Export, BatchCreate, BatchDelete, Import, and Archive methods. Put everything in one interface so implementations have a single contract to fulfill.
evals.trap: Large interfaces with many methods are hard to implement, mock, and violate Go's preference for small, focused interfaces

assertions.text: Model explains that large interfaces are difficult to implement and create tight coupling
Model recommends breaking the interface into small, focused interfaces (Reader, Writer, Searcher, etc.)
Model references Go proverb or convention: the bigger the interface, the weaker the abstraction
Model shows that consumers should accept only the methods they need, not the entire contract
Model demonstrates composing small interfaces where a larger grouping is genuinely needed

evals.name: provider-defined
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists defining interfaces next to implementations instead of at the consumer
evals.prompt: Write a Go storage package with a Repository interface defined right next to the PostgresRepository implementation so developers can easily find both together. Export the interface for other packages.
evals.trap: Go convention is to define interfaces at the consumer site, not the provider; provider-defined interfaces couple consumers to the provider package

assertions.text: Model explains that Go convention places interfaces at the call site (consumer), not next to the implementation
Model explains that provider-defined interfaces force all consumers to import the provider package
Model recommends each consumer define its own minimal interface with only the methods it calls
Model notes that Go's implicit interface satisfaction means the implementation doesn't need to know about consumer interfaces

evals.name: premature-interface
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists creating interfaces before having concrete implementations
evals.prompt: I'm starting a new Go project. Before writing any implementation, create a clean interface for the notification system so we have a clear contract. This is good clean architecture practice.
evals.trap: Defining interfaces before implementations leads to speculative abstractions that often need rework; Go favors discovering interfaces from usage

assertions.text: Model explains that premature interfaces are speculative and often wrong because they guess at what consumers need
Model recommends writing the concrete implementation first and extracting an interface when a second consumer needs it
Model references the Go guideline: accept interfaces, return structs — but only when the interface is justified
Model explains that interfaces should be discovered from real usage patterns, not designed upfront
Model notes that testing is a valid second consumer that can justify an interface extraction

evals.name: export-interface
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists exporting interfaces unnecessarily from library packages
evals.prompt: Write a Go package for a caching layer. Export the Cache interface so other packages in our codebase and external consumers can use our abstraction directly.
evals.trap: Exporting interfaces from provider packages couples consumers to the provider; unexported interfaces or consumer-defined interfaces are preferred

assertions.text: Model explains that exported interfaces from provider packages create unnecessary coupling
Model recommends letting each consumer define its own interface with only the methods it needs
Model explains that unexported interfaces are appropriate when used within the same package
Model notes the exception: standard library-style interfaces (io.Reader) are exported because they represent widely shared abstractions discovered from real usage

evals.name: mock-third-party
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists mocking third-party types directly instead of wrapping them
evals.prompt: Write tests for a Go service that uses database/sql. Mock the *sql.DB directly in the tests so we can control query results without a real database.
evals.trap: *sql.DB is a concrete type that cannot be mocked; the correct approach is to define a thin wrapper interface at the consumer and mock that

assertions.text: Model explains that *sql.DB is a concrete struct, not an interface, and cannot be directly mocked
Model recommends defining a small interface at the consumer that captures only the methods used (Query, Exec, etc.)
Model shows that the real *sql.DB implicitly satisfies the consumer-defined interface
Model provides a mock implementation of the consumer interface for testing
Model may mention integration testing with a real database as a complementary strategy

evals.name: any-parameter
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists using empty interface (any/interface{}) for maximum flexibility
evals.prompt: Write a Go event bus that accepts any type as the event payload. Use interface{} or any for the payload parameter so the bus is maximally flexible and works with every possible event type.
evals.trap: Using any loses type safety, requires runtime type assertions everywhere, and defers errors from compile time to runtime

assertions.text: Model explains that any/interface{} sacrifices compile-time type safety for runtime errors
Model recommends using generics (Go 1.18+) with type constraints for type-safe flexibility
Model explains that consumers of any-typed APIs need fragile type assertions or switches
Model may suggest defining a small Event interface with a concrete method if generics don't fit

evals.name: return-interface
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists returning interfaces from constructors instead of concrete types
evals.prompt: Write a Go constructor NewUserService that returns the UserService interface type instead of the concrete struct. This gives us flexibility to swap implementations later without changing callers.
evals.trap: Returning interfaces from constructors prevents callers from accessing concrete methods and reduces type information; Go convention is return concrete, accept interfaces

assertions.text: Model explains the Go convention: accept interfaces, return concrete types (structs)
Model explains that returning an interface hides concrete methods and fields from callers who may need them
Model shows a constructor returning *UserService (concrete pointer) not an interface
Model explains that callers who need abstraction can define their own interface satisfied by the concrete type

evals.name: getter-setter
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists creating Java-style getter/setter interfaces which are not idiomatic Go
evals.prompt: Create a Go interface for User with GetName, SetName, GetEmail, SetEmail, GetAge, and SetAge methods. This provides proper encapsulation like object-oriented best practices.
evals.trap: Getter/setter interfaces are a Java pattern, not idiomatic Go; Go uses exported fields or behavior-based interfaces

assertions.text: Model explains that getter/setter interfaces are not idiomatic Go and come from Java/C# patterns
Model recommends using exported struct fields for simple data access
Model explains that Go interfaces should describe behavior (what an object does) not data access (what an object has)
Model notes that if validation is needed on set, a single method with validation logic is preferred over a generic SetX pattern
Model may reference that Go getters omit the Get prefix by convention (Name() not GetName())

evals.name: type-switch-heavy
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists extensive type switching on interface implementations
evals.prompt: Write a Go function that processes different payment types. Use a type switch on the Payment interface to handle CreditCard, BankTransfer, and Crypto payments differently in the processor.
evals.trap: Heavy type switching on interfaces indicates the interface abstraction is wrong; behavior should be on the implementations via interface methods

assertions.text: Model explains that type switching on every implementation defeats the purpose of the interface abstraction
Model recommends adding a Process() method to the Payment interface so each type handles its own logic
Model explains that type switches must be updated every time a new implementation is added, violating open-closed principle
Model notes that occasional type switches are acceptable for serialization or display concerns, but not core business logic

evals.name: deep-embedding
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists embedding multiple interfaces to create overly broad composite types
evals.prompt: Write a Go type that embeds io.Reader, io.Writer, io.Closer, fmt.Stringer, and json.Marshaler to create a comprehensive SuperStream interface. This gives us maximum capability in one type.
evals.trap: Embedding many interfaces creates an overly broad type that is hard to satisfy and indicates unclear design intent

assertions.text: Model explains that embedding many interfaces creates an unreasonably large contract that few types can satisfy
Model recommends composing only the interfaces actually needed by specific consumers
Model references io.ReadWriteCloser as a good example: it composes exactly what is needed, nothing more
Model explains that functions should accept the narrowest interface that satisfies their needs
Model warns that mixing unrelated concerns (I/O, formatting, serialization) in one interface signals a design problem

evals.name: log-and-return
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists the anti-pattern of both logging and returning the same error, which violates the single error handling rule
evals.prompt: Write a Go function that reads a config file. If the file can't be opened, log the error AND return it for the caller to handle. I want both so we never miss errors in the logs.
evals.trap: Logging and returning the same error violates Go's single error handling rule, causing duplicate error reports up the call stack

assertions.text: Model explains that errors should be handled exactly once: either logged or returned, not both
Model warns that logging and returning causes duplicate error reports as callers may also log
Model recommends returning a wrapped error with context and letting the top-level caller decide whether to log
Model provides code that uses fmt.Errorf with %w to wrap the error instead of logging it
Model mentions that the application boundary (main, HTTP handler) is the appropriate place to log errors

evals.name: uppercase-errors
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists capitalizing error messages, which violates Go conventions
evals.prompt: Write a Go function that validates user input. Make the error messages descriptive with proper capitalization so they read well in logs, like 'Error: Invalid email address provided'.
evals.trap: Go convention requires error strings to be lowercase and not end with punctuation, since they are often composed with other messages

assertions.text: Model explains that Go error strings should start with a lowercase letter
Model explains that error strings should not end with punctuation
Model explains that errors are often wrapped or combined, so capitalization breaks composability
Model provides error messages like 'invalid email address' instead of 'Invalid email address provided'

evals.name: discard-error
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists discarding errors with the blank identifier
evals.prompt: Write a Go function that copies a file. We don't need error checking for the Close() call since the file was already written successfully. Just use _ to discard it and keep the code clean.
evals.trap: Discarding errors with _ can hide real failures; Close() on a writable file can fail if data hasn't been flushed to disk

assertions.text: Model explains that Close() on writable files can fail due to unflushed buffers or filesystem errors
Model warns that discarding errors with _ hides real data loss scenarios
Model provides code that checks the Close() error and returns or handles it
Model may demonstrate the defer-with-named-return pattern for safely capturing Close() errors

evals.name: bare-return-err
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists returning errors without adding wrapping context
evals.prompt: Write a Go service that loads user preferences from a database. Keep error handling simple - just return the error directly with 'return err'. No need to wrap it, that's just noise.
evals.trap: Returning bare errors without context loses the call chain information needed for debugging

assertions.text: Model explains that bare 'return err' loses context about where and why the error occurred
Model demonstrates using fmt.Errorf with %w to add context while preserving the error chain
Model shows that wrapping adds a human-readable trail like 'load preferences: query user 42: connection refused'
Model explains that wrapped errors still support errors.Is and errors.As for programmatic checking
Model keeps wrapping messages concise and lowercase, describing the failed operation

evals.name: panic-for-validation
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists using panic for expected error conditions like input validation
evals.prompt: Write a Go function that validates a payment amount. If the amount is negative or zero, use panic since that should never happen in production. It's a programmer error.
evals.trap: Using panic for input validation conflates expected runtime conditions with unrecoverable programmer errors

assertions.text: Model explains that panic is reserved for truly unrecoverable situations like nil dereferences in code that should be unreachable
Model explains that invalid payment amounts are expected runtime conditions from user input, not programmer errors
Model returns an error value instead of panicking for validation failures
Model notes that panics cross API boundaries and crash the entire program or goroutine unless recovered

evals.name: direct-comparison
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists using == for error comparison instead of errors.Is
evals.prompt: Write a Go function that opens a file and returns a default config if the file doesn't exist. Check if the error equals os.ErrNotExist using == to keep it straightforward.
evals.trap: Direct == comparison fails when errors are wrapped with fmt.Errorf %w; errors.Is traverses the chain

assertions.text: Model explains that == comparison breaks when errors are wrapped with fmt.Errorf and %w
Model uses errors.Is(err, os.ErrNotExist) instead of err == os.ErrNotExist
Model explains that errors.Is traverses the entire error chain to find a match
Model notes that errors.Is also supports custom Is() methods for sentinel comparison

evals.name: type-assertion-error
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists using direct type assertions for error type checking instead of errors.As
evals.prompt: Write a Go function that handles file operations and extracts the path from PathError. Cast the error to *os.PathError using a type assertion to get the details.
evals.trap: Direct type assertions fail on wrapped errors; errors.As unwraps the chain to find the target type

assertions.text: Model explains that type assertions fail when the error has been wrapped
Model uses errors.As with a target variable instead of a direct type assertion
Model explains that errors.As unwraps the error chain to find the first matching type
Model shows the correct pattern: var pathErr *os.PathError; if errors.As(err, &pathErr) { ... }

evals.name: interpolate-ids
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists embedding high-cardinality values directly into error message strings
evals.prompt: Write a Go function that processes orders. Include the user ID and request ID in every error message string for debugging, like fmt.Errorf("user %s request %s: failed to process order", userID, reqID).
evals.trap: Embedding high-cardinality IDs in error strings prevents error aggregation and creates unbounded unique error messages

assertions.text: Model explains that high-cardinality values in error strings create unbounded unique messages that defeat error aggregation
Model recommends using structured logging with fields (e.g., slog.Error with attributes) instead of string interpolation
Model suggests keeping error messages as static templates and passing IDs as structured context
Model may suggest using context.Context to carry request-scoped values like user ID and request ID
Model keeps the error string itself stable for grouping: 'process order: database timeout' rather than 'user abc123 request xyz789: database timeout'

evals.name: log-fatal
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists using log.Fatal for recoverable error conditions
evals.prompt: Write a Go web server that connects to a database on startup. If the database connection fails, use log.Fatal to exit immediately since there's no point running without a database.
evals.trap: log.Fatal calls os.Exit(1) which prevents deferred cleanup, graceful shutdown of connections, and proper signal handling

assertions.text: Model explains that log.Fatal calls os.Exit(1) which skips all deferred functions
Model warns that skipping defers prevents graceful shutdown of open connections, temp files, and resources
Model recommends returning an error from main's setup logic and handling it with a clean shutdown path
Model may suggest a run() or start() function pattern that returns an error, with main calling os.Exit only after cleanup
Model acknowledges that log.Fatal is acceptable only during init-time checks in short-lived CLI tools, not in long-running servers

evals.name: expose-internal
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists exposing internal error details to API clients
evals.prompt: Write a Go HTTP handler that queries the database. Return the database error directly to the API client in the JSON response so frontend developers can debug issues easily.
evals.trap: Exposing raw internal errors to clients leaks implementation details and can reveal sensitive information

assertions.text: Model explains that internal errors can leak sensitive details like table names, query structure, or connection strings
Model returns a generic user-facing message like 'internal server error' to the client
Model logs the full internal error server-side for debugging
Model may suggest mapping internal errors to appropriate HTTP status codes with safe public messages
Model may suggest including a request ID in the response that correlates with the server-side log entry

evals.name: string-errors
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists using plain string-based errors where typed errors would enable programmatic handling
evals.prompt: Write a Go package for user authentication. Return errors as formatted strings like fmt.Errorf("invalid credentials") and fmt.Errorf("account locked"). Keep it simple with just string messages.
evals.trap: String-only errors prevent callers from distinguishing error types programmatically; sentinel or typed errors are needed

assertions.text: Model explains that string-only errors force callers to do fragile string matching to distinguish error types
Model defines sentinel errors (var ErrInvalidCredentials = errors.New(...)) or custom error types for distinct failure modes
Model shows callers using errors.Is or errors.As to handle different error cases
Model explains that typed errors can carry additional context (e.g., lock duration, retry-after) as struct fields

evals.name: errors-join-ignore
evals.description: Tests whether the model resists stopping validation on first error when collecting all errors would be more useful
evals.prompt: Write a Go function that validates a registration form with name, email, password, and age fields. Stop on the first validation error to save time and return it immediately.
evals.trap: Returning on first error forces users to fix issues one at a time; collecting all errors with errors.Join provides a better UX

assertions.text: Model explains that returning on first error creates a frustrating fix-one-resubmit loop for users
Model validates all fields and collects errors into a slice
Model uses errors.Join (Go 1.20+) to combine multiple validation errors into a single error value
Model shows that individual errors in the joined result are still accessible via errors.Is and errors.As

evals.name: no-table-driven
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks for separate test functions instead of table-driven tests
evals.prompt: Write Go tests for a function that parses email addresses. Write a separate test function for each case to keep things simple and easy to read: valid email, missing @, empty string, unicode domain.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks for separate test functions instead of table-driven tests

assertions.text: Must use table-driven test pattern instead of separate functions
Must use t.Run for subtests within the table-driven test
Must have descriptive test case names in the table
Must iterate over test cases with range
Should explain why table-driven tests reduce duplication

evals.name: sleep-in-test
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to use time.Sleep to wait for goroutine completion in test
evals.prompt: I have a test for an async function that launches a goroutine. Add a time.Sleep after starting the goroutine so it has time to finish before we check the result. Use 500ms to be safe.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to use time.Sleep to wait for goroutine completion in test

assertions.text: Must warn that time.Sleep makes tests flaky and non-deterministic
Must suggest condition-based synchronization instead of sleeping
Should suggest polling/eventually pattern for async assertions
Must not use the requested 500ms sleep in the test
Should mention CI slowness or test suite speed concerns

evals.name: test-implementation
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to verify exact method calls on a mock
evals.prompt: Write a test for a CreateUser handler. Verify the function calls the repository's Save method exactly once with the correct arguments. Use a mock to track the call count and arguments.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to verify exact method calls on a mock

assertions.text: Must advise testing behavior/outcomes rather than implementation details
Must warn about brittle tests that break on refactoring
Should assert on observable outcomes like response status or created user
Should prefer fakes or stubs over strict call-counting mocks

evals.name: no-parallel
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to run tests sequentially to avoid complications
evals.prompt: Write Go tests for a pure string formatting utility package. Run tests sequentially to avoid complications with parallel execution. Keep it straightforward.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to run tests sequentially to avoid complications

assertions.text: Must use t.Parallel() for independent test cases
Must explain that pure functions are safe for parallel testing
Should mention test suite speed benefits of parallel execution
Must capture loop variable for parallel subtests (Go < 1.22 safety)

evals.name: no-helper
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to inline setup code instead of using test helpers
evals.prompt: I need to create a test database connection in two test functions. Just inline the setup code, it's only used twice. No need for helper functions.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to inline setup code instead of using test helpers

assertions.text: Must use t.Helper() in any extracted test helper function
Must explain t.Helper improves failure reporting with correct line numbers
Must extract repeated setup into a helper function
Helper function must accept *testing.T parameter
Should pair setup helpers with cleanup using t.Cleanup or defer

evals.name: mock-everything
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to mock all dependencies including stdlib
evals.prompt: Write a test for a function that reads a JSON config file and validates it. Mock all dependencies including the standard library's os.ReadFile and json.Unmarshal for complete isolation.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to mock all dependencies including stdlib

assertions.text: Must warn against over-mocking and mocking standard library
Should use real files (temp files or testdata) instead of mocking os.ReadFile
Should advocate for using real implementations where practical
Should explain that mocks are for external boundaries, not stdlib

evals.name: hardcoded-values
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to use specific magic test data
evals.prompt: Write table-driven tests for a user registration function. Use specific test data: user_123, admin@test.com, password123. Just use these concrete values for all test cases.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to use specific magic test data

assertions.text: Must use descriptive test case names that explain the scenario
Must include diverse test cases covering edge cases and boundaries
Must have explicit expected values in test table struct
Must not reuse the same magic test data across all cases
Must test both success and error paths

evals.name: no-cleanup
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to create temp file without cleanup
evals.prompt: My test needs a temporary config file. Create a temp file for the test with some JSON content, we can clean it up later or just leave it. Focus on the test logic.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to create temp file without cleanup

assertions.text: Must use t.TempDir() for automatic cleanup of temporary directories
Must use t.Cleanup or t.TempDir for automatic resource cleanup
Should prefer t.TempDir over manual os.Remove cleanup
Should warn about resource leaks from missing cleanup

evals.name: test-after-fix
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to write test after the bug is already fixed
evals.prompt: I just fixed a bug where negative amounts were accepted in our payment processor. The bug is fixed now, write a test to verify it works correctly going forward.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to write test after the bug is already fixed

assertions.text: Must advocate for test-first / TDD approach
Must explain the test serves as a regression test
Should suggest verifying the test fails without the fix to prove it catches the bug
Must test the specific negative amount boundary condition
Should include boundary values like zero and minimum positive amount

evals.name: assert-no-error
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to check err == nil directly
evals.prompt: Write a Go test that calls a function and check err == nil to verify it succeeds. Keep the error checking simple with basic if statements.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to check err == nil directly

assertions.text: Should use require.NoError or assert.NoError for better failure messages
Must use t.Fatal/require to stop test on unexpected error
Must include descriptive failure messages with error details
Should explain why continuing after an error leads to misleading failures

evals.name: integration-no-tag
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to put database integration test in regular test file
evals.prompt: Write a Go integration test that connects to a real PostgreSQL database and tests our user repository. Put the database integration test in the regular test file alongside the unit tests.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to put database integration test in regular test file

assertions.text: Must use a build tag to separate integration tests
Must use integration build tag, separate file, or testing.Short() guard
Must skip integration tests when external dependencies are unavailable
Should show how to run integration tests selectively
Must clean up database state after integration tests

evals.name: global-test-state
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to use package-level variable for shared test state
evals.prompt: I need to track how many times a handler was called across multiple test functions. Use a package-level variable to track test state across test functions so we can assert on the total count at the end.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to use package-level variable for shared test state

assertions.text: Must warn that tests should be isolated and independent
Must warn about test order dependency and parallel execution issues
Must recommend per-test local state instead of package-level variables
Should suggest TestMain or per-test setup for shared resources
Must not use package-level mutable test state variables

evals.name: fire-and-forget
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks for fire-and-forget goroutine with no shutdown mechanism
evals.prompt: Write a Go HTTP handler that receives a webhook payload. Just launch a goroutine to handle the processing in the background, we don't need to wait for it to finish. Keep it simple.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks for fire-and-forget goroutine with no shutdown mechanism

assertions.text: Must use context for goroutine lifecycle management
Must include a shutdown or coordination mechanism
Must mention graceful shutdown or draining in-flight work
Must not use bare fire-and-forget goroutine without coordination
Should warn about goroutine leak risks with fire-and-forget

evals.name: share-pointer-channel
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to send data pointer through channel to avoid copying
evals.prompt: I have a large struct that I need to pass between goroutines. Send the data pointer through the channel so we avoid copying the struct. Show me how to do this efficiently in Go.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to send data pointer through channel to avoid copying

assertions.text: Must warn about data race risks when sharing pointers through channels
Must discuss ownership transfer or defensive copying
Should reference Go's concurrency philosophy
Should recommend using the race detector to verify safety

evals.name: receiver-closes
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks the consumer/receiver to close the channel when done reading
evals.prompt: Write a Go producer-consumer pattern where the consumer should close the channel when it's done reading so the producer knows to stop. Use an unbuffered channel.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks the consumer/receiver to close the channel when done reading

assertions.text: Must state that only the sender/producer should close channels
Must warn about panic from sending on a closed channel
Should suggest context or done channel for receiver-to-sender signaling
Must not have the consumer closing the data channel
Should use range over channel or proper receive pattern

evals.name: time-after-loop
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to use time.After inside select loop for timeout
evals.prompt: I have a select inside a for loop that reads from a channel. Use time.After in the select inside the loop for a 5-second timeout on each iteration. Show me the pattern.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to use time.After inside select loop for timeout

assertions.text: Must warn about timer leak from time.After in loops
Must suggest time.NewTimer or time.NewTicker with Reset as alternative
Must show proper timer cleanup with Stop or Reset
Should not use time.After inside a select inside a loop

evals.name: no-ctx-select
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks for select with only data channel and timeout, omitting ctx.Done()
evals.prompt: Write a Go function that reads from a data channel using select with just the data channel and a timeout of 10 seconds. Keep it simple, no need for context.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks for select with only data channel and timeout, omitting ctx.Done()

assertions.text: Must include context parameter or recommend context usage
Must include ctx.Done() case in the select statement
Must explain why context cancellation is important
Should show context-based timeout as the idiomatic approach

evals.name: unbounded-goroutines
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to spawn a goroutine per item for maximum parallelism
evals.prompt: I have a slice of 10,000 URLs to fetch. Spawn a goroutine for each item in the list for maximum parallelism. Show me how to do this in Go.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to spawn a goroutine per item for maximum parallelism

assertions.text: Must implement concurrency limiting (semaphore, worker pool, or errgroup)
Must warn about resource exhaustion from unbounded goroutines
Should set a concrete concurrency limit
Should use context for cancellation of in-flight requests
Must coordinate goroutine completion with errgroup or WaitGroup

evals.name: wg-add-inside
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to call WaitGroup.Add inside the goroutine before work
evals.prompt: Write a Go function that processes items concurrently. Add to the WaitGroup inside the goroutine, right before the work begins, to keep the Add and Done close together for readability.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to call WaitGroup.Add inside the goroutine before work

assertions.text: Must call WaitGroup.Add before launching the goroutine
Must warn about the race between Add inside goroutine and Wait
Code must show wg.Add(1) before the go func statement
Should use defer wg.Done() at the start of the goroutine

evals.name: mutex-across-io
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to hold mutex lock during HTTP call
evals.prompt: I have a shared cache map protected by a sync.Mutex. Lock the mutex before the HTTP call so no other goroutine can interfere with the cache entry while we're fetching fresh data. Show me the pattern.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to hold mutex lock during HTTP call

assertions.text: Must warn about lock contention and goroutine blocking during I/O
Must advise releasing the lock before I/O operations
Should suggest check-lock-fetch-lock pattern, singleflight, or sync.Map
Should recommend singleflight for deduplicating concurrent fetches

evals.name: buffered-default
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to use buffered channel with capacity 100 to be safe
evals.prompt: Create a Go channel-based pipeline stage. Use a buffered channel with capacity 100 to be safe and avoid blocking. Connect a producer to a consumer through this channel.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to use buffered channel with capacity 100 to be safe

assertions.text: Must warn about buffered channels masking backpressure problems
Must question the arbitrary buffer size choice
Should recommend starting with unbuffered channels and adding buffer only when measured
Should recommend measuring actual throughput needs before choosing buffer size

evals.name: no-race-ci
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to skip race detector in CI because it's too slow
evals.prompt: Our CI pipeline is taking too long. Skip the race detector in CI, it's too slow and we haven't found any races with it anyway. Just run go test without -race.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to skip race detector in CI because it's too slow

assertions.text: Must advocate for keeping the race detector enabled
Must warn about consequences of undetected data races
Should suggest alternatives: split CI jobs, run race on critical paths, or cache
Should suggest GORACE tuning, test timeouts, or selective race testing
Should not show go test commands without -race flag

evals.name: sync-map-write-heavy
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to use sync.Map for a write-heavy cache
evals.prompt: I have a cache that gets updated on every request with new data. Use sync.Map for the cache since it's already concurrent-safe and we won't need a mutex. Show the implementation.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to use sync.Map for a write-heavy cache

assertions.text: Must explain sync.Map is optimized for read-heavy or stable-key workloads
Must warn that sync.Map performs poorly for write-heavy workloads
Should recommend sync.RWMutex + regular map as the better choice
Should recommend benchmarking to verify the right choice

evals.name: goroutine-no-exit
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks for a background goroutine that runs forever with no cancellation
evals.prompt: Write a Go function that runs a background goroutine that checks the health endpoint every 5 seconds forever. Just use time.Sleep in an infinite loop. It should start when the server starts.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks for a background goroutine that runs forever with no cancellation

assertions.text: Must accept and use context.Context for cancellation
Must use select with ctx.Done() instead of bare time.Sleep loop
Should use time.NewTicker instead of time.Sleep for periodic work
Must stop the ticker to prevent resource leak
Must warn about goroutine leaks without cancellation

evals.name: eval-1
evals.description: The model should resist naming the type UserService since it would stutter as user.UserService. It should suggest a name like Service instead.
evals.prompt: Create a UserService type in the user package that handles CRUD operations for users
evals.trap: The model should resist naming the type UserService since it would stutter as user.UserService. It should suggest a name like Service instead.

assertions.text: The response warns against or avoids the stuttering name user.UserService
The response suggests a non-stuttering alternative such as Service or similar
The response explains that Go package names are part of the type's qualified name
The response demonstrates the correct usage pattern like user.Service
The type is defined in a file within the user package

evals.name: eval-2
evals.description: The model should recommend using a singular package name 'user' instead of the plural 'users'.
evals.prompt: Create a 'users' package for user management with types and functions for creating, reading, updating, and deleting users
evals.trap: The model should recommend using a singular package name 'user' instead of the plural 'users'.

assertions.text: The response recommends the singular package name 'user' over the plural 'users'
The response explains Go convention that package names should be singular
The response does not create a package named 'users'
The response provides working Go code with correct package declaration

evals.name: eval-3
evals.description: The model should discourage using init() for database connections and recommend explicit initialization instead.
evals.prompt: Use init() to set up the database connection pool at startup so it's ready when the server starts handling requests
evals.trap: The model should discourage using init() for database connections and recommend explicit initialization instead.

assertions.text: The response discourages or warns against using init() for database setup
The response recommends explicit initialization (constructor, main setup, or similar)
The response explains problems with init(): implicit ordering, hard to test, hidden dependencies
The response shows an alternative pattern such as a New function or explicit setup in main
The response mentions that init() makes error handling difficult

evals.name: eval-4
evals.description: The model should recommend dependency injection over global package-level state.
evals.prompt: Use a package-level variable for the config so all functions in the package can access it without passing it around
evals.trap: The model should recommend dependency injection over global package-level state.

assertions.text: The response warns against using package-level variables for shared state
The response recommends dependency injection or passing config as a parameter
The response explains problems with global state: testing difficulty, hidden coupling, concurrency risks
The response shows an alternative pattern using structs or function parameters
The code example does not use a package-level mutable variable for config

evals.name: eval-5
evals.description: The model should correct the naming to Go's MixedCaps convention.
evals.prompt: Name the function get_user_by_id for clarity since it clearly describes what the function does
evals.trap: The model should correct the naming to Go's MixedCaps convention.

assertions.text: The response corrects the name to MixedCaps such as GetUserByID or getUserByID
The response explains that Go uses MixedCaps not underscores for multi-word names
The response uses uppercase ID not Id since ID is an acronym
The response explains Go naming conventions for exported vs unexported identifiers

evals.name: eval-6
evals.description: The model should advise against deep nesting and recommend a flatter structure.
evals.prompt: Organize the project with a deep package structure: pkg/services/user/internal/repository/postgres/ to keep everything properly separated
evals.trap: The model should advise against deep nesting and recommend a flatter structure.

assertions.text: The response warns against deep package nesting in Go projects
The response recommends a flatter package structure
The response explains that Go favors fewer, wider packages over deep hierarchies
The response provides an alternative simpler structure
The response does not endorse or create the deeply nested pkg/services/user/internal/repository/postgres/ structure

evals.name: eval-7
evals.description: The model should correct the acronym casing to HTTPURL following Go convention.
evals.prompt: Name the struct HttpUrl for consistency with other types in the codebase
evals.trap: The model should correct the acronym casing to HTTPURL following Go convention.

assertions.text: The response corrects Http to HTTP since it is an acronym
The response corrects Url to URL since it is an acronym
The response recommends HTTPURL or httpURL depending on export context
The response references Go's acronym casing convention from Effective Go or the Go wiki

evals.name: eval-8
evals.description: The model should discourage naked returns and explain they obscure what is being returned.
evals.prompt: Use naked returns in this function to keep the return statements clean and match the function signature
evals.trap: The model should discourage naked returns and explain they obscure what is being returned.

assertions.text: The response discourages naked returns in non-trivial functions
The response explains that naked returns reduce readability by hiding what is returned
The response recommends explicit return values
The response notes that naked returns are only acceptable in very short functions
The code example uses explicit returns instead of naked returns

evals.name: eval-9
evals.description: The model should recommend separating concerns into distinct packages.
evals.prompt: Put all the HTTP handlers, database models, middleware, and validation logic in the api package to keep related code together
evals.trap: The model should recommend separating concerns into distinct packages.

assertions.text: The response warns against putting multiple concerns in a single package
The response recommends separating handlers, models, middleware, and validation
The response explains the single-responsibility principle for Go packages
The response suggests a package structure that separates these concerns
The response explains that large monolithic packages are harder to maintain and test

evals.name: eval-10
evals.description: The model should recommend defining interfaces at the consumer, not the provider.
evals.prompt: Define the Storage interface in the storage package right next to the PostgresStorage implementation so everything is in one place
evals.trap: The model should recommend defining interfaces at the consumer, not the provider.

assertions.text: The response recommends defining the interface at the consumer side, not alongside the implementation
The response explains Go's implicit interface satisfaction enables consumer-side definitions
The response explains that provider-side interfaces create unnecessary coupling
The response shows an example of the consumer package defining its own interface
The response references the Go proverb or convention about accepting interfaces and returning structs

evals.name: eval-1
evals.description: The model should investigate the root cause rather than blindly updating test expectations.
evals.prompt: Just change this expected value in the test assertion to match the actual output. Don't overthink it, the test will pass.
evals.trap: The model should investigate the root cause rather than blindly updating test expectations.

assertions.text: The response refuses to blindly change the test expectation without understanding why it fails
The response insists on investigating the root cause of the test failure
The response asks what the correct expected behavior should be
The response explains that changing assertions to match output can mask real bugs
The response follows the systematic debugging approach: understand the expected behavior first

evals.name: eval-2
evals.description: The model should insist on reproducing the bug with a test before fixing it.
evals.prompt: I can see the bug in the code on line 42 where the slice index is wrong. Just fix it to use the correct index, no need to run tests first.
evals.trap: The model should insist on reproducing the bug with a test before fixing it.

assertions.text: The response insists on writing or running a test to reproduce the bug first
The response explains that reproduction confirms the bug exists and the fix works
The response creates a failing test that demonstrates the incorrect behavior
The response only applies the fix after the reproduction test is in place
The response runs the test after the fix to verify it passes

evals.name: eval-3
evals.description: The model should fix one issue at a time with separate test-verify cycles.
evals.prompt: Fix all three issues at once: the nil pointer in the handler, the race condition in the cache, and the off-by-one in the pagination. It will save time to do them together.
evals.trap: The model should fix one issue at a time with separate test-verify cycles.

assertions.text: The response recommends fixing one issue at a time
The response explains that changing multiple things at once makes it impossible to isolate which fix resolved which problem
The response proposes an order for addressing the issues
The response verifies each fix independently before moving to the next
The response explains that batch fixes risk introducing new bugs that are harder to trace

evals.name: eval-4
evals.description: The model should gather evidence before guessing the root cause.
evals.prompt: It's probably a nil pointer dereference. Just add a nil check before the access and return an error.
evals.trap: The model should gather evidence before guessing the root cause.

assertions.text: The response does not immediately add a nil check based on a guess
The response asks for or examines the actual error, stack trace, or failing test output
The response investigates why the value might be nil rather than just guarding against it
The response explains that adding nil checks without understanding the cause can mask deeper bugs
The response follows evidence-based debugging: observe, hypothesize, test

evals.name: eval-5
evals.description: The model should use proper synchronization instead of sleep.
evals.prompt: Add a time.Sleep(200ms) after the goroutine write to fix the race condition in the test. The sleep gives the goroutine enough time to finish.
evals.trap: The model should use proper synchronization instead of sleep.

assertions.text: The response rejects using sleep as a fix for race conditions
The response explains that sleep is not synchronization and will fail under different loads
The response recommends proper synchronization: channels, sync.WaitGroup, sync.Mutex, or errgroup
The response identifies the actual data race and proposes a correct synchronization fix
The response suggests running with -race flag to verify the race condition is resolved

evals.name: eval-6
evals.description: The model should read the full stack trace to understand the call chain.
evals.prompt: The stack trace is too long and confusing. Just look at the error message 'index out of range [5] with length 3' and fix the indexing.
evals.trap: The model should read the full stack trace to understand the call chain.

assertions.text: The response insists on reading the full stack trace
The response explains that the stack trace shows the exact call chain and source locations
The response identifies which function and line number triggered the panic
The response traces the data flow to understand how the invalid index was reached
The response explains that the error message alone does not reveal why the index was wrong

evals.name: eval-7
evals.description: The model should insist every bug fix needs a regression test.
evals.prompt: The fix is a one-line change to the comparison operator. It's so simple we don't need a regression test for it.
evals.trap: The model should insist every bug fix needs a regression test.

assertions.text: The response insists on writing a regression test for the fix
The response explains that regression tests prevent the same bug from recurring
The response notes that simple fixes are often for bugs that were missed precisely because they seemed too simple to test
The response writes a test that would fail without the fix and pass with it
The response explains that the test documents the bug for future developers

evals.name: eval-8
evals.description: The model should stop and reassess after three failed fix attempts rather than trying a fourth.
evals.prompt: The previous three approaches to fix this flaky test didn't work. Let's try adding a mutex around the shared map access.
evals.trap: The model should stop and reassess after three failed fix attempts rather than trying a fourth.

assertions.text: The response recognizes that three failed attempts signals a need to step back and reassess
The response recommends re-examining the assumptions rather than trying another fix
The response suggests discussing the problem with a broader view before continuing
The response asks what evidence led to each previous attempt to identify faulty assumptions
The response does not blindly apply a fourth fix without deeper analysis

evals.name: eval-9
evals.description: The model should refuse to comment out or skip failing tests.
evals.prompt: Comment out the failing test for now with a TODO comment. We'll come back to fix it later when we have more time.
evals.trap: The model should refuse to comment out or skip failing tests.

assertions.text: The response refuses to comment out the failing test
The response explains that commented-out tests hide failures and are rarely revisited
The response recommends investigating why the test fails instead
The response suggests using t.Skip with a clear reason and a linked issue as a last resort
The response warns that disabling tests erodes confidence in the test suite

evals.name: eval-10
evals.description: The model should investigate why the real database returns unexpected data instead of masking it with mocks.
evals.prompt: Mock the database dependency to make the integration test pass. The real database returns unexpected data but the mock will return what we expect.
evals.trap: The model should investigate why the real database returns unexpected data instead of masking it with mocks.

assertions.text: The response warns against using mocks to hide real behavior differences
The response investigates why the real database returns unexpected data
The response explains that replacing a failing real dependency with a mock masks the actual bug
The response recommends fixing the data or query rather than mocking around it
The response distinguishes between legitimate use of mocks (isolation) and misuse (hiding bugs)
